segments.s102-g49: چنین is an adverb/demonstrative placed at the start ('such/thus'), followed by the copula است ('is') to form a fixed evaluative phrase. کردار is the nominal predicate ('conduct, deed') and is followed by the genitive/attributive phrase گردون پیر ('the ancient/old heaven') where پیر is an adjective after the noun; the whole clause states a general truth about the ways of the heavens. The present copula است is simple indicative; the clause prepares the comparison that follows.
segments.s102-g50: This is a paired, contrasting construction: گهی ... و گاهی ... ('sometimes ... and sometimes ...') marks alternation. چون/چو introduce similes ('like/as'), and کمانست is a poetic fusion of کمان + است ('is a bow' or 'is like a bow') with the copula attached to the noun. The second half repeats the pattern with چو تیر ('like an arrow'), so the whole segment specifies two alternating states of the previously mentioned 'conduct of heaven.'
segments.s102-g17: به + قلب اندرون forms a locative phrase 'into/at the inner heart' (به = toward/into; قلب اندرون = 'the inner heart/innermost court'). 'قارن رزم‌زن' is a name followed by an epithet: the proper name قارن (here a personal name) is immediately followed by the descriptive compound رزم‌زن ('battle‑striker/warrior') — Persian often places such epithets after the name.
segments.s102-g18: This is a sequence of names/titles presented in apposition: اباگرد کشواد appears as a (compound) proper name and is then qualified by لشگر شکن 'breaker of the host(s)'. گرد and ابا here form part of the name (not the ordinary preposition or noun), and لشگر شکن is a noun + agent 'breaker' describing him.
segments.s102-g19: پس پشت‌شان = 'then/behind them' (پس = then/so; پشت‌شان = their rear/behind them). زال با کیقباد places two names in coordination: زال (the hero Zāl) followed by با + کیقباد ('with Kay Qobād'). The particle با marks accompaniment.
segments.s102-g20: The repeated pattern 'به یک دست ... به یک دست ...' is instrumental: 'with one hand ... with one hand ...' The preposition به here has an instrumental/associative force ('by/with'), یک دست = 'one hand', so the line means they held 'fire in one hand, wind in the other.'
segments.s102-g21: به پیش اندرون = 'before/in front (in the inner place)' (به + پیش giving a locative 'in front of'); کاویانی درفش = 'the Kayanian standard' (کاویانی modifies درفش). Word order: locative phrase first, then the noun phrase naming the standard.
segments.s102-g22: جهان زو شده = 'the world has become from it' — ز is the poetic 'from/because of' and the following clitic (here written separately) refers back to the banner ('from it'). شده = 'has become'. The colors listed (سرخ و زرد و بنفش) are predicates linked by و 'and' describing the resultant hues.
segments.s102-g23: ز لشکر چو کشتی سراسر زمین is a simile: ز لشکر 'from/coming the host' (ز = from/because of), چو = 'like/as', کشتی = 'a ship', سراسر زمین = 'throughout the land' (saraasar = entire/throughout + زمین = land). Word order: simile marker چو follows the noun it compares.
segments.s102-g24: کجا موج خیزد ز دریای چین is a rhetorical question: کجا = 'where', موج خیزد = 'would a wave rise' (خیزد = subjunctive/present 'rise, appear'), ز دریای چین = 'from the sea of folds/wrinkles' (دریای = 'sea of', چین = 'folds/wrinkles' — a figurative image). The verb خیزد is the present/subjunctive-type classical form 'rises/would rise'.
segments.s102-g33: میان سپاه = a locative phrase 'in the midst (middle) of the army' (میان functioning as 'middle'). اندر آمد = an older/poetic way to say 'entered' (اندر = in/within + آمد = came, past). دلیر follows the verb as a postposed nominal predicate/subject ('the brave one'), a common option in older Persian narrative.
segments.s102-g34: سپهدار قارن = title + proper name in apposition ('the commander Qaran'). به کردار = a prepositional phrase 'in the manner/behavior of' where به is used adverbially/relationally ('by/‑ly' sense) and کردار = 'conduct/manner'. شیر is the comparison term 'lion', a metaphor describing his bearing.
segments.s102-g35: گهی ... گهی = an adverb repeated to indicate alternation ('sometimes ... sometimes'). سوی چپ / سوی راست = directional phrases 'toward the left' and 'toward the right' (سوی = toward/side + چپ/راست indicating left/right). The repetition emphasizes shifting direction.
segments.s102-g36: بران گونه = 'in that manner' (بران = 'upon/thereon' i.e. 'in that', گونه = 'manner/way'). از هر سویی = 'from every side/quarter' (از = from, هر = every, سویی here 'side/direction'). کینه خواست = verb phrase with object before verb: کینه (hatred/grudge, here 'vengeance/hatred') + خواست (past 'wanted/sought') — 'he sought vengeance'.
segments.s102-g37: A list of instrumental phrases introduced by به used to mark the weapons ('with' / 'by means of'): به گرز (with a mace), و به تیغ (and with a sword), و سنان دراز (and a long spear). دراز postmodifies سنان (adjective after noun) producing 'long spear'.
segments.s102-g38: همی کشت = narrative imperfective/continuous sense 'he kept slaying' or 'he continually slew' (همی is an older continuous marker). از ایشان = 'of them' / 'from them' (object phrase). گو سرفراز = 'thus called Sarpraz' where گو functions as an older/poetic 'called/so‑said' and سرفراز is an epithet 'victorious / proud'.
segments.s102-g39: ز (poetic form of از) کشته = 'with/consisting of the slain' (کشته as noun 'the slain'). زمین کرد = 'made the ground' (کرد = caused/made — here 'he made the earth [appear]'). مانند کوه = simile 'like a mountain', completing the idea that the slain piled up like a mountain.
segments.s102-g40: شدند = past plural 'they became' (change of state). آن دلیران ترکان = 'those brave Turks' (آن = that/those; دلیران = the brave ones; ترکان identifies their group). ستوه = predicate adjective 'weary / exhausted' describing their state after the slaughter.
segments.s102-g1: به شاهی نشست is a classical phrasing meaning 'assumed the kingship' (literally 'sat to kingship'); the verb نشست (past) indicates he took the seat. از برش here means 'in his place/from his seat' (برش = 'his place/position' in context), and کیقباد is the subject (proper name) who performs the action.
segments.s102-g2: همان تاج گوهر به سر برنهاد is a single clause: همان ('that' / 'the very') modifies تاج گوهر ('jeweled crown'); برنهاد is the past tense verb 'placed upon' (بر + نهاد), and به سر marks the goal 'on his head'. Word order is object/phrase before verb, typical of Persian poetry.
segments.s102-g3: همه نامداران شدند انجمن uses شدند ('became') to mean 'gathered' or 'formed' here: 'all the famed ones became an assembly' — نامداران functions as a plural substantive ('the renowned'). انجمن is the result noun 'assembly/meeting'.
segments.s102-g4: چو ... و چون ... is a comparative 'as/like ... and as/like ...' linking the named examples to the previous clause; دستان and قارن are proper names (heroes) here, so they function as examples of the 'renowned ones', and رزم‌زن is a descriptive compound 'battle-striker / warrior'.
segments.s102-g5: چو introduces a temporal/comparative 'when/as', followed by a list of three names joined by و. کشواد and برزین are personal names; خراد is presented as an epithet meaning 'wise' (used here as a name/qualifier). گو at the end is the suffix '-speaking' or an epithet-forming element attached to برزین (a poetic name-form).
segments.s102-g6: فشاندند is the past plural 'they scattered/sprinkled'; its object گوهر ('jewels') precedes the locative phrase بران ('upon that') and the head تاج نو ('new crown'). The verb-final order and the plural verb agreement indicate multiple agents performing the action.
segments.s102-g7: قباد از بزرگان سخن بشنوید is an instance of classical verb morphology: بشنوید is used as a past/perfect form here meaning 'he heard' (classical -ید ending). از بزرگان ('from the great ones') marks the source, and سخن ('speech/words') is the thing heard.
segments.s102-g8: پس ... بدید links events: پس ('then') introduces the following action; افراسیاب و سپه ('Afrasiab and the host/army') are the direct objects (سپه is marked by را), and بدید is the past tense 'he saw'. The object marker را highlights سپه as a definite direct object.
segments.s102-g25: سپر در سپر بافته is a poetic verbless clause describing interlocked shields: سپر (shield) + در (here 'in/among', a prepositional link) + سپر (repeated for the idiom ‘shield upon shield’) + بافته (past-participle used adjectivally ‘woven/interwoven’). دشت و راغ follows as a nominative pair (‘the plain and [râgh]’) coordinating two nouns with و. The whole segment lists the battlefield surface/setting after the image of shields.
segments.s102-g26: درفشیدن تیغها چون چراغ is a clause where درفشیدن (a verbal form meaning ‘press in/force together’) governs تیغها (pl. ‘swords’) as its object; چون چراغ is a simile ‘like lamps’. The verb is pastive/participial in tone (poetic narrative), so read as ‘the swords pressed/closed in, like lamps (they shone)’. The order is verb–object–simile typical of Persian poetic compression.
segments.s102-g27: جهان سر به سر گشت is an idiomatic phrase: جهان ‘the world’ + سر به سر گشت ‘became all over/throughout’ (سر به سر = ‘entirely’). دریای قار immediately follows as a predicate nominal, ‘a sea of قار’; قار here names the quality of the sea (sense: loud/harsh cry), so the line means the world became a sea of clamour. گشت is the past tense copulative/verb ‘became’.
segments.s102-g28: برافروخته شمع ازو صدهزار is a compact clause where برافروخته (participle ‘kindled/ablaze’) modifies شمع (candles). ازو ‘from it’ marks source, and صدهزار ‘a hundred thousand’ quantifies the candles. Word order is adjective–noun–source–numeral, a condensed poetic sequence meaning ‘a hundred thousand candles were kindled from it’.
segments.s102-g29: ز نالیدن بوق و بانگ سپاه is a prepositional phrase introduced by ز (poetic 'from' / 'because of') that links the following soundscape to the scene: نالیدن ‘wailing’ governs the instruments/voices listed (بوق و بانگ) and سپاه (‘the army’) specifies whose sounds they are. The noun sequence uses the genitive/source relation implied by ز.
segments.s102-g30: تو گفتی که خورشید گم کرد راه is a reported-speech clause. تو is the 2nd-person subject ‘you’; گفتی is the past-tense verb ‘said’. که introduces the content clause ‘the sun lost its way’: خورشید (subject) + گم (adjective/participle ‘lost’) + کرد (auxiliary ‘did/caused’ used here to form a past action) + راه (object ‘way’). The construction گم کرد راه = ‘lost the way’.
segments.s102-g31: سبک قارن رزم‌زن کان بدید is an introductory nominal + verb phrase with epithets: سبک ‘light/agile’ modifies the name قارن (here a proper name, given as an epithet) and رزم‌زن ‘warrior’ is an appositive descriptor. کان is an archaic connective meaning roughly ‘when/that/thereupon’ and بدید is the past-tense verb ‘he saw’. The order puts the epithets before the verb in typical heroic poetic style.
segments.s102-g32: چو رعد از میان نعره‌ای برکشید is a simile + action: چو ‘like/as’ introduces the simile (رعد ‘thunder’). از میان ‘from among/the midst of’ specifies source, نعره‌ای ‘a cry/shout’ is the thing produced, and برکشید is the past tense ‘he lifted/raised’ (here raising a cry). Word order is simile — source phrase — object — verb, a compressed poetic sequence meaning ‘he raised a shout like thunder from the midst’.
segments.s102-g41: شماساس را دید گرد دلیر follows a classical Persian order where the object with the accusative marker را (شماساس را) precedes the verb دید; the post‑verbal phrase گرد دلیر functions as the subject (a proper name + epithet: “Gird the brave”). را marks شماساس as definite/accusative; دید is simple past ‘saw’.
segments.s102-g42: که می‌برخروشید چون نره شیر is a relative clause introduced by که ‘who/that’ modifying شماساس. می‌برخروشید is the imperfective/continuous form (present stem with the mi‑ prefix) meaning ‘was roaring/kept raging’. چون + نره شیر is a simile ‘like a male lion’. The clause is subordinate and describes Shamasas’ action.
segments.s102-g43: بیامد دمان تا بر او رسید is a two-part motion clause: بیامد (simple past ‘came’) plus the subject/name دمان (here following the verb), then تا introduces a limit clause ‘until’ (تا بر او رسید = until he reached him). بر here is directional ‘toward/to’, او is the third‑person pronoun, and رسید is past ‘reached/arrived’.
segments.s102-g44: سبک تیغ تیز از میان برکشید is a compressed action: سبک تیغ تیز = ‘a light, sharp sword’ (سبک modifying تیغ and تیز as an attributive adjective). از میان ‘from between’ gives the source/location, and برکشید is the past form ‘drew up / drew (a weapon)’ (برکشیدن used for drawing a sword). Word order places adjectives before the noun as modifiers.
segments.s102-g45: بزد بر سرش تیغ زهر آبدار is built around the past verb بزد ‘he struck’. بر سرش (‘upon his head’) uses بر + noun + enclitic pronoun ش to give the target of the blow. تیغ زهر آبدار is a noun phrase ‘a sword (تیغ) soaked/charged with poison’; آبدار is used figuratively as a modifier ‘poison‑drenched/poisonous’. The sequence places the weapon phrase after the target phrase.
segments.s102-g46: بگفتا منم قارن نامدار is direct reported speech introduced by the past narrative verb بگفتا ‘he said’. منم is the emphatic copular ‘I am’, followed by the name/title قارن and the adjective نامدار ‘renowned/illustrious’. The clause is straightforward declarative speech in past narrative context.
segments.s102-g47: نگون اندر آمد شماساس گرد is an idiomatic resultative: نگون (‘overthrown/downfallen’) + اندر آمد (‘came into/entered’), giving the sense ‘was cast down’ or ‘fell’. شماساس گرد is the name (Shamasas Gird) appearing after the verb phrase; classical Persian often allows subject/name after the predicate for emphasis.
segments.s102-g48: چو دید او ز قارن چنان دست برد begins with the temporal conjunction چو ‘when’. دید او ‘he saw’ places the pronoun after the verb (typical in older Persian). ز قارن = ‘from Qaran’ (ز used poetically as ‘from/at the sight of’), چنان ‘thus/in that way’, and دست برد is an idiomatic verbal phrase: دست (hand/effort) + برد (past of بردن ‘to take / withdraw / put forth’ here) meaning ‘withdrew his hand’ or ‘let go’. The whole clause: ‘when he saw Qaran, he so withdrew his hand.’
segments.s102-g9: دگر روز ('the next/another day') is a temporal phrase; برداشت is a past tense verb meaning 'lifted/took up' used here with لشکر as its agent (poetic word order puts the verb before or adjacent to the noun). ز جای is a prepositional phrase 'from its place/camp' completing the verb phrase 'برداشت لشکر ز جای' = 'the army rose/moved from its place.'
segments.s102-g10: خروشیدن آمد is a light-nominal + verb construction: the infinitive/gerund خروشیدن ('roaring/clamor') with آمد ('came') yields 'a roar came' or 'clamor arose.' ز introduces the source 'from,' and پرده‌سرای is the source/place phrase ('curtained hall' / 'stage/scene').
segments.s102-g11: بپوشید is the classical past 3sg ('he put on' / 'he donned'); in this clause the proper name رستم is the agent and سلیح نبرد ('weapon/armament of battle') is the object (genitive/attributive juxtaposition). Poetic order places the verb before or next to the subject and its object.
segments.s102-g12: چو here functions as 'when/as'; the phrase پیل ژیان شد is 'became an elephant of life' where ژیان ('life, vigor') qualifies the image (i.e., mighty as a living elephant). که introduces the following clause and برخاست گرد is a compound action 'rose up' with گرد functioning adverbially ('around/about' / turning/bringing motion into focus).
segments.s102-g13: رده بر کشیدند is a fixed poetic expression: کشیدند ('they drew') with the adverbial/prepositional بر ('on/upon') and رده ('ranks/rows') gives 'they drew up in ranks/formed ranks.' ایرانیان is the plural subject, placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s102-g14: ببستند is the past plural 'they closed/shut' (here used idiomatically to mark the onset/engagement of fighting). خون ریختن is an infinitive/gerund phrase 'the shedding/ spilling of blood'; را is the definite object marker attaching to that nominalized action, and میان ('among/between') gives the spatial relation. Overall the clause conveys that bloodletting began among them (poetic usage lets ببستند take the nominalized action as its object).
segments.s102-g15: به یک دست is an idiom meaning 'on one hand / on one side' (یک 'one' is part of the phrase though omitted from the provided word list). مهراب کابل خدای is an appositive: the proper name مهراب followed by کابل (the place) and خدای used as a title ('lord/master' in older/political usage rather than literal 'God'). The phrase places the name and his epithet together.
segments.s102-g16: دگر دست = 'the other hand/side.' گژدهم is a proper name; جنگی is an adjective/noun meaning 'warlike' or 'fighter' describing him; به پای is a prepositional phrase 'on foot / at the foot' (literal 'to/at foot'), placing his characteristic or position. Poetic inversion places epithets after the name.

sentences.s102-s25: Such is the conduct of the ancient heaven: at times it is like a bow and at times like an arrow.
sentences.s102-s9: In the inner heart stood Qaran the warrior; Abagird Keshvad, breaker of hosts, was all around.
sentences.s102-s10: Behind them, Zāl with Kay Qobād — in one hand fire, in one hand wind.
sentences.s102-s11: Before them, the Kayanian standard in the inner front — the world because of it became red and yellow and purple.
sentences.s102-s12: From that army, like a ship across the whole land — where could a wave rise from the sea of folds?
sentences.s102-s17: Into the midst of the host strode the brave commander Qaran, in the manner of a lion.
sentences.s102-s18: Sometimes to the left and sometimes to the right — in that fashion he sought vengeance from every quarter.
sentences.s102-s19: With mace and with sword and a long spear he ever slew them; thus he was called Sarpraz (the Victorious).
sentences.s102-s20: He heaped the slain on the ground like a mountain; those brave Turks became weary.
sentences.s102-s1: Kay Qobad took the kingship in his stead and placed that jeweled crown upon his head.
sentences.s102-s2: All the renowned men formed an assembly, like Dastān and like Qāran, warriors in battle.
sentences.s102-s3: When Kushvād and Kharād and Barzin‑go did so, they scattered jewels upon that new crown.
sentences.s102-s4: Qobad heard the words of the great ones; then he saw Afrasiab and the army.
sentences.s102-s13: Shields interlocked, shield upon shield; across the plain and longing, the swords pressed in, like lamps.
sentences.s102-s14: The whole world was turned into a sea of clamor; from it a hundred thousand candles were kindled.
sentences.s102-s15: From the wailing — the army's trumpets and cries — you said that the sun had lost its way.
sentences.s102-s16: Swift Qaren, the warrior, when he saw that, like thunder from the midst he lifted a cry.
sentences.s102-s21: Gird the brave saw Shamasas, who was roaring like a male lion.
sentences.s102-s22: Daman came up until he reached him; he drew a light, sharp sword from between [them].
sentences.s102-s23: He struck at his head with a poison‑drenched sword and said, “I am Qaran the famed.”
sentences.s102-s24: Shamasas Gird was overthrown; when he saw Qaran, he withdrew his hand thus.
sentences.s102-s5: The next day the army broke camp; a roar came from the curtained hall.
sentences.s102-s6: Rostam donned his war-gear; when he became like a living elephant, he rose up and turned about.
sentences.s102-s7: The Iranians drew up in ranks; blood began to be shed among them.
sentences.s102-s8: On one side Mehrab, lord of Kabul; on the other hand Gozdahm, a warlike fighter on foot.
